section.name: dotfiles-structure
section.summary: Git dotfiles repository layout, GNU stow symlink mechanism, per-category packages, and machine-specific override pattern.

rule.statement: Organize dotfiles as per-category stow packages (bash/, git/, tmux/, vim/, ssh/) so individual packages can be installed on any machine independently.
rule.rationale: A VPS needs bash, git, tmux, and ssh config but not vim GUI plugins or workstation browser bookmarks. Package isolation lets bootstrap pick only what is relevant to the machine type.

rule.statement: Mirror the $HOME directory structure inside each stow package; stow creates symlinks at $HOME that point into the package directory.
rule.rationale: stow uses the directory tree under each package as a template for $HOME. A file at dotfiles/bash/.bash_aliases creates ~/.bash_aliases as a symlink. This is stow's core design — match the target directory tree.

example.code:
# Install stow sudo apt install -y stow
# From the dotfiles root, stow individual packages cd ~/dotfiles stow bash # Creates ~/.bashrc, ~/.bash_aliases, etc. stow git tmux vim # Multiple packages at once
# Dry run before stowing (see what will happen) stow -n -v bash
# Restow after editing a config (remove + recreate symlinks) stow -R tmux
# Unstow (remove symlinks for a package) stow -D vim
# Handle an existing file: backup first, then stow mv ~/.bashrc ~/.bashrc.backup stow bash

antipattern.description: Using stow --adopt to absorb existing files into the package without reviewing the result.
antipattern.reason: --adopt moves the existing file into the stow package, potentially overwriting your dotfiles repo version. Always review git diff after --adopt to confirm the absorbed content is what you want to keep.

rule.statement: Keep machine-specific config in separate stow packages (machine-server/, machine-workstation/) rather than branching the git repo per machine.
rule.rationale: Branches require merging and diverge over time. Separate stow packages on the same branch can coexist on any machine; only the relevant one is stowed at bootstrap time. The repo stays in sync across all machines.

example.code:
# machine-server/.bashrc.d/server.sh — loaded only on servers export EDITOR=vim alias services='systemctl --user list-units --type=service' alias health='bash /srv/nero/health-check.sh'
# machine-workstation/.bashrc.d/workstation.sh — loaded only on workstations export EDITOR=code alias open='xdg-open'
# bootstrap.sh: detect machine type and stow the right package MACHINE_TYPE="server" # or detect from hostname, GUI presence, etc. stow "machine-$MACHINE_TYPE"

section.name: modular-bashrc

rule.statement: Use a .bashrc.d/ directory pattern sourced by .bashrc so machine-specific files, tool integrations, and aliases can be added/removed without editing the main .bashrc.
rule.rationale: A monolithic .bashrc becomes hard to maintain across machines. .bashrc.d/ enables modular addition: each stow package can add its own .bashrc.d/*.sh file without conflicting with others.

example.code: # ~/.bashrc — sources all .bashrc.d/ files if [ -d "$HOME/.bashrc.d" ]; then for f in "$HOME/.bashrc.d"/*.sh; do [ -r "$f" ] && source "$f" done unset f fi

section.reference: Full bootstrap script: clone dotfiles, install stow, backup conflicts, stow packages, detect machine type
